# Questions in Livestream 203

Live Stream #203: Degenerative Artificial Intelligence

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=693" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:33</a> | How can a late-date Exodus view handle Merneptah's campaign overlapping Joshua's era? | He said the overlap is not fatal because Merneptah targeted places like Gezer while Israel was still concentrated farther inland around Shiloh. | He argued that Merneptah's campaign has to intersect either Joshua or Judges on any Exodus chronology, so the question is which setting explains it better. In his late-date reading, Merneptah was not making a broad tribute circuit but punishing Gezer after instability following Ramesses II; Israel had only recently entered Canaan, had focused first on Amorite cities, and was gathered around Shiloh while tribal land questions were negotiated. That makes the Israel encounter peripheral rather than a major battle that Joshua would need to narrate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=1184" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:44</a> | Were Amorites mentioned in Merneptah's stele? | No. He said the stele mentions groups such as the Hurrians and remnants of Mitanni, but not the Amorites, who were not being treated as Egyptian enemies there. | He corrected the premise directly: the Amorites are not named in Merneptah's stele. The groups he identified instead were the Hurrians and the Kuru, whom he connected with remnants of Mitanni. He explained that Amorites were not the target of conquest in that text because they were not being presented as Egyptian enemies in the same way, and he noted that the region's factions and alliances are easy for lay readers to confuse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=1285" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:25</a> | Why do you favor the conspiracy-assassination reading of Ay and Horemheb's succession? | He pointed especially to the Dakhamunzu affair as the reason he leans that way. | He said the Dakhamunzu affair best fits the pieces: an Egyptian royal widow asked Suppiluliuma I to send a son to become king, the Hittite prince was killed by Egyptian chariots in Canaan, and that kind of diplomatic assassination implies direction from someone powerful. In his reconstruction, Ay and Horemheb were the viable contenders after Tutankhamun; Horemheb was the field general who could intercept the prince, while Ay buried Tutankhamun and took the throne. The scenario also explains later political intrigue, including Ay's sons disappearing before Horemheb ultimately benefited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=1636" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:16</a> | What does it mean to incorporate a Christian worldview into fiction? | He argued for embedding a moral vision in the story itself rather than turning fiction into a sermon. | Drawing on John Gardner's _On Moral Fiction_, he said all fiction carries a moral vision through theme, premise, and character, whether the author intends it or not. A Christian worldview can appear through claims about human nature, God's nature, sin, honesty, consequences, and similar moral patterns rather than explicit preaching. He contrasted that with unchristian moral premises and urged readers to look for the worldview a novel enacts, since themes are usually embodied by the story rather than stated outright. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=2004" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:24</a> | Are there Egyptian texts that treat Ramesses and Avaris as separate sites, and what would that imply? | Yes. He said they are genuinely separate places, divided by both distance and water, which matters for Exodus-site arguments. | He argued that Avaris and Pi-Ramesses were separate by ancient standards because they were about two kilometers apart and separated by water, with Avaris on the south bank and Pi-Ramesses on an island. He pointed to textual shifts from Avaris as a city to "the port of Avaris," and to the port's special role as the area's deep-water port even after the city itself was abandoned. For Exodus dating, he said this makes a wide-scale abandonment of Avaris most plausible during the reign of Ramesses II rather than much earlier. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=2520" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:00</a> | In Luke 23:43, does "today" mean immediately, soon, or just someday? | He treated the saying as pointing to immediate postmortem fellowship rather than an indefinite someday. | He read "today" as meaning that same day, not an undefined future. He explained paradise as the realm of the blessed dead, whether one calls it Abraham's bosom, the good side of Sheol, or heaven more broadly. His point was that Christ died before the thieves, went ahead of them, and promised the repentant thief fellowship in paradise that day. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=2709" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:09</a> | Does Exodus 21 support abortion by treating an unborn child as not yet a person? | No. He said the passage concerns an accidental miscarriage case and legal uncertainty, not fetal personhood. | He said the appeal to Exodus 21 is a misuse because the passage concerns two men fighting, a pregnant woman being struck after getting involved, and a resulting miscarriage whose direct cause may not be legally clear. The fine is tied to uncertainty over causation, not to a claim that the unborn child lacks humanity. If further injury is identifiable, the lex talionis principle applies, so he treated the passage as legal clarification rather than a statement about abortion or fetal personhood. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=3059" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">50:59</a> | What is good advice for leading a Bible study? | He recommended reading and understanding the text first, using commentaries, and avoiding a study that becomes shared uninformed impressions. | His advice was basic but pointed: know what the text actually says before leading others through it. He recommended reading the passage, consulting commentaries, and trying to understand the text rather than merely asking everyone what they feel about it after a first reading. He warned that without preparation, a Bible study can become a circle of shared ignorance instead of study. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=3369" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:09</a> | What is the strongest argument for the resurrection, and which argument needs the most tweaking? | He said this is outside his specialty, but the early church's resurrection belief from the beginning is probably the strongest line he would point to. | He prefaced the answer by saying resurrection apologetics is not his area and that specialists such as Gary Habermas would be better sources. Still, he suggested the earliest Christian tradition's belief in the resurrection from the beginning, along with claims that people saw the risen Christ, is stronger than the common "would die for a lie" argument. He called the latter weaker because people can die for things they wrongly believe, even if they are sincere. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=3593" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:53</a> | If ancient Hebrew originally wrote mainly consonants, how do we know how names were pronounced? | He said the issue is not a total absence of vowels but how vocalization is recovered through tradition, comparative evidence, and language history. | He corrected the idea that ancient Hebrew had no vowels: the language had vowels, but early writing did not record them the way later Masoretic pointing did. He explained that the Tiberian and Babylonian pointing systems preserve later vocalization traditions, while comparative Semitic evidence helps test likely patterns. He was generally confident in the broad Masoretic patterns, especially where they resemble vowel patterns in languages such as Akkadian, but he allowed that specific vowels may have shifted over time. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=3819" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:39</a> | Why did Philistines attack Israel, and were they trying to conquer, settle, or secure trade routes? | He said the conflict was chiefly over agricultural land in the Shephelah, not trade routes or conquest for its own sake. | He described the Israelite-Philistine conflict as an agricultural war over the Shephelah, the fertile strip between the Judean foothills and the coastal plain. Israelite fortified sites lay to the east and Philistine fortresses to the west, so both sides pressed toward the same valuable land. He emphasized that the soil was especially fertile because erosion from the iron-rich foothills washed into the valley, making food production the main issue rather than trade routes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=3974" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:14</a> | Was Samson burning the fields a war crime because he destroyed wheat and orchards? | He treated it as part of the same agricultural-war context, where destroying crops attacked the resource the conflict was about. | He connected Samson's destruction of fields to the same Shephelah land conflict. Since the war was about arable land and food, burning wheat and orchards attacked precisely what the Philistines and Israelites were contesting. He did not give a modern legal analysis of war crimes; he framed the act as intelligible within an agricultural war over scarce fertile land. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=4119" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:39</a> | Was Nimrod a Hebrew name for Gilgamesh, or were they different people? | He said Nimrod is probably a literary composite or amalgamation, not simply Gilgamesh or one identifiable person. | He argued that Nimrod is better understood as a literary composite, where multiple figures or traditions are compressed into one narrative character. He compared this to the Pharaoh of the oppression in Exodus 1, where many royal actions can be treated as a single literary unit for narrative economy. Because Nimrod is credited with associations across cities and origins that do not fit one historical person neatly, he warned against tying him simply to Gilgamesh or any single figure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=4287" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:27</a> | Why does 1 Kings 16:23 say Omri ruled twelve years if your chronology has six? | He said Omri's first years were contested in civil war; the six-year figure reflects independent or complete rule. | He explained the difference as contested versus complete rule. Omri came to power amid a civil war connected with the previous royal house, so some of the twelve years counted in Kings overlap with a contested period. A chronology may therefore show about six years for independent control, or show the contested years as overlapping with rival reigns. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=4432" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:52</a> | What is the major significance of the Dead Sea Scrolls, and are there recommended books? | He pointed to the specialist Discoveries in the Judaean Desert series and suggested Emanuel Tov for text-critical work, while noting it is not a casual topic. | He said his own use of the Dead Sea Scrolls is specialized, mainly for textual criticism, so he did not recommend casual reading from personal experience. For primary work he pointed to the expensive multi-volume _Discoveries in the Judaean Desert_, noting it gives texts and transliterations rather than easy lay translations. He also suggested Emanuel Tov for text-critical work and mentioned large scholarly projects that compare the Dead Sea Scrolls with other textual traditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=4782" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:42</a> | Is the book of Job possibly mytho-history? | Yes. He said Job is poetic and can be read as mytho-history, but that does not mean it did not happen. | He said Job is clearly structured as poetic narrative and that reading it as mytho-history is reasonable, comparable in broad genre terms to poetic mytho-history like the Iliad. He added that mytho-history does not mean the event did not happen; it means the material is received and presented in a heightened literary way, including interaction with divine beings. He also advised reading Job when life is going well, because it addresses people with prosperity and security. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=4911" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:51</a> | When Yahweh defeated Egypt in the Exodus, was that a message to other nations and their gods? | He said the question assumes too much; he does not think the plagues were mainly about defeating the Egyptian pantheon, but about a deeper cultural message to Egypt. | He treated the wording as a complex question because it assumes the plagues were primarily about Yahweh defeating Egypt's gods. He said he does not think that is the main point, and he criticized surface-level attempts to map each plague directly onto the pantheon. In his view, the plagues carried a more deeply cultural message to Egypt, including the claim that the Egyptian pantheon was effectively nothing before Yahweh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=5145" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:45</a> | How should faith and hope be distinguished among the divine virtues? | He said faith is trust, belief, and fidelity, while biblical hope is better understood as expectation rather than modern uncertainty. | He defined faith through Greek _pisteuo_ categories: intellectual assent, trust, and fidelity. Hope was the trickier term because modern English often implies uncertainty, while older English and the biblical sense he described mean expectation. Thus faith in Christ and hope in his return are not the same thing: faith is trusting and remaining faithful, while hope is confident expectation that what is promised will occur. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=5563" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:43</a> | Is Eve's name related to a second tense form like Yahweh? | He said it is possible and interesting, but more work on Hebrew second-tense possibilities would be needed. | He did not settle the proposal but called it a possible and interesting observation. He said more research is needed into whether Hebrew has second-tense phenomena comparable to other Mediterranean languages, and he thought the question could be a useful example for that kind of study. His answer stayed tentative because he did not have time to do that research himself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=5929" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:49</a> | Where are the ethical boundaries for student use of AI, fair use, and cheating? | He compared AI to Wikipedia: it may give a starting point, but using it to do the research or write the paper undermines the assignment and can become cheating. | He said AI can provide a starting point, much like Wikipedia, but a student should not stop there or let it replace the work assigned. If AI writes the paper, that is clear cheating; if it performs the research, it may be harder to catch but still undermines the purpose of learning to research. He also warned that students often cannot judge whether AI's sources are good, so he saw its current value as a learning tool as limited and easy to misuse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=6372" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:12</a> | How should someone help other Christians view topics through the lens of God's holy nature? | He said God's holiness is central, but urged caution: what is revelatory to one person may not be persuasive or wise to press on everyone else. | He agreed that holiness is a central attribute of God and can reshape how someone thinks about God and the problem of evil. However, he cautioned that a truth that transforms one person may not be ready or persuasive for someone else, and some arguments are not worth forcing. His advice was to express the view considerately and casually where appropriate, but not to assume being right makes pressing the point wise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=6792" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:12</a> | What was the process for Moses to get an audience with Pharaoh? | He said Moses would go through palace handlers and an anteroom, and as an adopted royal family member he had standing to seek an audience. | He described an Egyptian court process in which someone approached palace handlers, presented credentials such as a summons, diplomatic status, or royal-family status, and then waited in an anteroom before admission to the king. Some petitioners could be refused, but Moses' adoption into the royal family would give him a right to be heard. That status did not make the audience automatic, but it made access plausible within court procedure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=6991" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:31</a> | Would Moses still count as royal family if he had left Egypt before his adopted grandfather became Pharaoh? | Yes. He said leaving the country would not annul the adoption into the Ramesside clan, though the status would not be especially high. | He said adoption into the Ramesside clan would not be cancelled merely because Moses left Egypt. It was not like losing a visa status after being absent from the country; he remained an adopted member of the family. Still, he qualified that Moses' rank would be low or distant, not close to the throne. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=7083" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:03</a> | Does Pharaoh's daughter's adoption of Moses set up his later access to Pharaoh? | Yes. He said Moses had standing with Israel, with the Egyptian court, and with Yahweh, which made his role in the story coherent. | He said the adoption gives Moses the kind of dual status that helps explain his role in the Exodus narrative. He compared it with figures in Ramesside labor-dispute texts who could speak to workers, appeal to officials, and use elite connections. Moses similarly had standing with Israel as one of them, standing at court through adoption, and standing before Yahweh, which made his negotiating role coherent rather than artificial. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=7429" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:49</a> | What are your thoughts on the Gavin versus Dan McClellan dispute over biblical monotheism? | He rejected an early-polytheism reading of the biblical text and distinguished the Old Testament's descriptions of Israelite polytheism from what it prescribes. | He rejected the claim that the biblical texts themselves teach an early polytheism, saying he sees monarchial monotheism in the text. He acknowledged that many Israelites practiced polytheism, but stressed that the Old Testament describes that behavior negatively rather than prescribing it. His main interpretive distinction was between narrative description of Israelite sin and theological endorsement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=7705" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:08:25</a> | If salvation is only through Christ, but those who do not know him can still be saved, what is the point of evangelism? | He said evangelism is not pointless because it calls those who do not know or have rejected Christ into right relationship with God before it is too late. | He separated people into those who accept Christ, those who reject him, and those who never knew him. Evangelism matters because it informs the ignorant and calls rejecters to repentance while there is still opportunity. He allowed that the fate of those who never knew is not explicit and may involve God's grace and justice, but he rejected the idea that this makes evangelism pointless. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=7969" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:49</a> | Would God offer a second chance to someone who rejected false teachings about Christ? | He said that case is speculative: rejecting only a false Christ without ever receiving the true teaching is a maybe, but embracing a false Christ is different. | He distinguished rejecting false teaching about Christ from embracing a false Christ or a different gospel. Someone who only rejected a distorted presentation and never received the true teaching may be a "maybe," but he said the issue is speculative and would be weighed by God's perfect justice, holiness, and grace. He treated active embrace of a false Christ as a much more serious and settled category. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=8098" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:14:58</a> | Is it a problem that Exodus does not mention the pyramids or the Sphinx? | No. He said this is an argument from silence; pyramids were obsolete by the Exodus period and not relevant to the Israelites' location or labor. | He called the objection an old argument from silence. In his dating, the Exodus falls in the 19th Dynasty, long after pyramid building had become obsolete, and the Israelites were not living or laboring around the pyramids. He allowed that some Egyptian texts mention the Sphinx in special contexts, but said ordinary texts, including Egyptian ones, often omit famous monuments when they are irrelevant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=8618" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:38</a> | Who created ideas such as temples, sacred space, and ritual purity? | He said these seem to have been widespread ancient Near Eastern ideas about divinity rather than inventions traceable to one person. | He said these concepts seem to be general ancient Near Eastern patterns rather than inventions of one identifiable person. Shared ideas about divinity produced similar practices around temples, sacred space, and purity across many groups. He dated the spread of such ideas very early, at least to the third or fourth millennium BC, while admitting we cannot specify exactly how or where they began. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=8744" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:44</a> | Do sacred space and temples still matter since Christians have Jesus and the Holy Spirit? | He said biblical prophecy still anticipates temple and sacred-space language, but repurposed toward praise and worship rather than sin offerings. | He answered from a millennial-kingdom perspective, saying biblical prophecy still includes temple and sacred-space realities. In that setting, however, the temple's role would not be forgiveness of sin or sin offerings but thanksgiving, praise, and worship. He described it as a repurposing of temple practice in a positive direction rather than a return to sacrifices that cover sin. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=9324" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:24</a> | Do Jews usually use an early or late date for the Exodus? | He said most Jewish dating is closer to a late date, often around 1312 BC, though some Jewish readers influenced by evangelicalism hold early dates. | He said most Jewish or rabbinic dating places the Exodus around 1312 BC, which is much closer to the late-date range than to the early-date range. He treated 1446 to 1406 BC as the ordinary early-date range, with anything after about 1400 falling outside early-date territory. He allowed that some Jewish readers hold early dates, but attributed that partly to evangelical influence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=9668" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:41:08</a> | Did learning Japanese kanji, hiragana, or katakana help with Akkadian cuneiform? | No. He said Japanese writing was much easier and more pictographic, while Akkadian cuneiform lacks those helpful visual clues. | He said Japanese did not help him with Akkadian because the writing systems work very differently. Kanji can retain pictographic clues and kana are clearly phonetic, which makes Japanese easier to orient in. Akkadian cuneiform, by contrast, looks visually similar across signs, includes ideograms and determinatives that do not stand out clearly, and requires working sign by sign without the same pictographic help. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=9904" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:04</a> | What would be the scariest thing to find on Mars? | He said life would be scariest because unknown biology could raise major questions about origin, danger, contamination, and epigenetic change. | He said life would be frightening because it would introduce questions that ordinary Martian geology does not: where it came from, whether it is dangerous, and how it might interact with human physiology or Earth's ecosystems. He especially emphasized unknown contamination and epigenetic change, using grasshoppers turning into locusts as an example of how life can alter under changed conditions. Unknown Martian organisms brought to Earth could therefore pose unpredictable risks. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=10148" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:49:08</a> | Is the Exodus Pharaoh's tomb helpful to apologetics? | No. He said royal tombs present only favorable royal material; sources such as Late Egyptian Miscellanies are more useful for Exodus-related context. | He said neither Amenhotep II's tomb nor Ramesses II's tomb would be very useful apologetically, because royal tombs are designed to display accomplishments and praise, not embarrassing material. He preferred sources such as the Late Egyptian Miscellanies, student-copy texts from the Ramesside period that preserve legal and cultural details. Those texts, in his view, provide more useful Exodus-related context than any particular Pharaoh's tomb. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=10327" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:07</a> | How can true randomness and God both exist if God knows what will happen? | He said this confuses categories: God's knowledge of an outcome does not negate randomness or determine the event. | He identified the objection as a modal scope fallacy. In his explanation, an event can be random because no created cause is controlling it, while God can still know its outcome because he knows the future. Knowledge of the result is not the same thing as placing influence on the event. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=10557" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:57</a> | If God knows the outcome of every event, does that determine every event? | No. He said God's omniscience and middle knowledge include actual outcomes and contingencies without necessarily causing each choice. | He said God's omniscience includes not only actual events but also contingencies, which he described as middle knowledge. As an example, he referred to God warning David what would happen if he went to a certain place, even though David then avoided that outcome. God's knowing the path someone will take, and knowing what would happen on other paths, does not mean God causes each choice. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=10838" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:00:38</a> | If God is outside time, how can he change anything or act? | He said God can intrude into time and history because he is sovereign over time, even though his essence is outside time. | He answered that being outside time does not prevent God from entering or acting within time. God's essence can be outside history while God still intrudes into history, such as at Sinai, to affect events. He linked this to God's ontological priority and sovereignty over time itself. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=10928" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:08</a> | What is your interpretation of the binding of Isaac in Genesis 22? | He said the passage is deeply debated; his view is that God required Abraham to offer Isaac but that stopping the sacrifice was always part of the plan. | He stressed that Genesis 22 is one of the most debated Old Testament passages and that interpreters differ sharply. His own view is that Abraham was commanded to offer Isaac, but that the wording does not require God to have intended the sacrifice to be completed; the stopping was part of the plan. He also highlighted Abraham's "we will return" statement and the experiential knowledge theme: the test manifests Abraham's fear of God before Isaac and others, not merely in theory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=11705" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:05</a> | How certain are we that the Egyptian loan words in Exodus are really Egyptian? | He said confidence is high because the words can be compared across Semitic and Egyptian language histories, with some terms long predating their entry into Hebrew. | He said scholars can compare the histories of Semitic languages such as Akkadian and Aramaic with Egyptian to see which words belong to which language family. For Egyptian loanwords, the key is that many terms have a long Egyptian history before they enter Hebrew or other Semitic use; he used Egyptian linen terminology as an example. He also noted that the process works in both directions, since Semitic words can enter Egyptian too, so the evidence for genuine Egyptian loans in Exodus is strong. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=12011" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:11</a> | Are there any more updates on the Thutmose II tomb? | He said no major updates are expected soon because the tomb was heavily damaged by flooding, though the final report may eventually add details. | He said he was not aware of further updates and did not expect much immediately because the tomb suffered severe water and flood damage. He explained that the Valley of the Kings is a wadi, so rare rain can send destructive floods through tomb entrances, leaving mud and silt that damage reliefs and objects. The final report might contain something interesting, but the surviving material sounded limited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=12417" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:26:57</a> | Are there restrictions on bringing water bottles to Egyptian sites? | No. He said visitors are expected to bring bottled water, but should keep control of it inside tombs and monuments. | He said visitors can generally bring bottled water to Egyptian sites and are expected to do so because of the heat and because local water is not advisable for visitors. The caution is practical and preservation-related: keep control of the bottle inside tombs, pyramids, and monuments. He recounted a bottle rolling down a pyramid shaft and breaking as the kind of mishap to avoid. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=12588" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:48</a> | Did you see Kip's response about the alleged couplet in Numbers 23:7-10? | No. He said he had not seen it and did not see how the passage would support the claimed documentary-hypothesis point. | He said he had not seen the response and did not intend to spend much time on it. After reading Numbers 23:7-10, he said he did not understand how the passage was supposed to function as a couplet in a documentary-hypothesis argument. His answer was mainly that he did not see the claimed point in the text and did not consider the source worth extended attention. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=12721" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:01</a> | Who is more credible, Billy Carson or Ken Hovind? | He said neither is credible and compared the choice to choosing between two poisons. | He rejected both as credible sources. He grouped Hovind with figures who keep attracting new audiences despite repeated debunking, and he used Ron Wyatt as another example of an internet-amplified following outliving the original claims. The substance of the answer was that neither should be treated as reliable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=12978" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:36:18</a> | Did Egypt's sorcerers and magicians have demonic power, or was the serpent sign a magic trick? | He said the Bible does not specify; Egyptians practiced magic, but the method in Exodus could range from tricks to ritual magic or other explanations. | He said the Bible does not tell us what method the Egyptian magicians used. Egyptian priests certainly practiced magic, and some practices may have involved stage-like tricks, ritual magic, applied pharmacology, communal psychology, or other mechanisms. Because the text does not specify whether demonic power was involved, he refused to choose one explanation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=13197" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:57</a> | Was baby Moses being brought out of the water a hint of Hebrews coming out of water chaos? | He thought that was probably overinterpretation, but accepted it as foreshadowing of the Sea of Reeds rather than a chaos motif. | He said the chaos-water reading is probably too much because Moses is placed among reeds in the calm part of the river, not sent into violent water. He saw the stronger connection as foreshadowing: Moses is put among reeds and drawn out, while later Israel passes through the Sea of Reeds. He also noted that the Nile is generally wide and calm except in contexts such as inundation, so the immediate scene does not work well as a chaos motif. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=13606" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:46:46</a> | Is there an indicator that Aaron was older or younger than Moses? | Yes. He said Aaron is presented as three years older than Moses. | He said the textual indication is that when Moses is eighty, Aaron is said to be eighty-three. That makes Aaron the older brother by three years. He did not expand the answer beyond that basic chronological marker. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=13655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:47:35</a> | Is it safe to swim with Nile crocodiles? | No. He said it is never safe, though ancient people took risks and more often bathed than swam. | He answered flatly that it is never safe. He clarified that ancient Egyptians more often bathed than swam, and people tried to avoid especially dangerous areas, though they did take risks. He also noted that both crocodiles and hippos were present in the Nile, and reed patches might be safer than open banks but not enough to make crocodiles safe. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/6DKyKGhC8Q4?t=13791" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:51</a> | Why did Ehud turn around at the stone images near Gilgal instead of killing Eglon immediately? | He said Ehud was using the idol setting as a ruse to claim a secret divine message, get Eglon alone, and make escape possible. | He explained that Ehud presented tribute before the idols and then used that setting to pretend he had a secret divine message for Eglon. Killing Eglon immediately in public would have left Ehud surrounded by Eglon's attendants with no clean escape. By claiming a private message from the god, he got Eglon alone, delivered the assassination, and bought time to get away. |
